---
title: "Software setup"
subtitle: "R, RStudio, TinyTeX, Quarto, and optional Git"
toc: true
---

This page is the **reference summary** of the software stack used in
this course. For a **step-by-step walkthrough** with verification
commands, see [Lab 1 — Install the stack](../labs/lab01-install-stack.qmd).

If you only have ten minutes, read Lab 1 first.

## The required stack

| Tool | What it does | Where to get it |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **R** | The language. Computation, simulation, plotting. | <https://cran.r-project.org> |
| **RStudio** | The editor. Friendly UI for writing R and Quarto. | <https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/> |
| **TinyTeX** | A minimal LaTeX distribution, installed via R. Lets Quarto render PDFs and lets you compile `.tex` files. | Install from R: `install.packages("tinytex"); tinytex::install_tinytex()` |
| **Quarto** | The reproducible-document engine. The connective tissue of this course. | <https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/> |

The four together are enough to do every required piece of work in
the course.

## Optional

| Tool | When you'd want it |
|------|---------------------|
| **Git** | If you plan to use a GitHub (or other) repository for your portfolio. Not required. |
| **VS Code** | An alternative editor if you prefer it to RStudio. Quarto has a good VS Code extension. |
| **A CAS** | See [CAS options](cas-options.qmd) for the optional bridge homework. |

## Verifying the install

Open your system terminal (Terminal on mac/Linux, PowerShell on
Windows) and run:

```bash
R --version
quarto --version
```

Both should print version strings. In RStudio's Console:

```r
tinytex::tinytex_root()
```

should print a path to your TinyTeX install.

## Folder convention

Pick a folder for the course and **don't move or rename it**:

```
math-software-portfolio/
├── hw01/
│   ├── hw01.qmd
│   └── hw01.pdf
├── hw02/
├── ...
├── latex-project/
├── r-project/
└── reflection.qmd
```

Keep paths inside your `.qmd` files **relative** to that folder. That
way the project renders on any computer, including mine when I grade.

## Common gotchas

- **First PDF render fails on a fresh TinyTeX.** Run `quarto render`
  a second time — TinyTeX downloads missing LaTeX packages on demand
  the first time it sees them.
- **`quarto` not on PATH.** Restart your terminal after installing
  Quarto. On Windows, you may need to restart RStudio too.
- **R chunk doesn't run in Quarto.** Make sure the fence is
  `` ```{r} `` (lowercase r, no spaces inside the braces).
- **PDF compiles but math is missing.** You probably wrote `\$x\$`
  instead of `$x$`. Drop the backslashes.
- **Mac asks for permission to access folders.** macOS sandboxing can
  block R from reading certain folders. Keep your portfolio under
  `~/Documents/` and you'll avoid this.

## What about Posit Cloud / Quarto Pub?

Both exist and both can host the work you do in this course:

- **Posit Cloud** — RStudio in a browser. Useful if your computer
  can't install R locally. Free tier has CPU-hour limits.
- **Quarto Pub** — free publishing for Quarto sites. Useful only if
  you specifically choose to publish your portfolio.

Neither is required. Use them if they help.
